 Digital inequality and neighborhood divides in online social networks in Chicago 

[Download PDF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y.pdf)

[Download PDF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y.pdf)

*   Article
*   [Open access](https://www.springernature.com/gp/open-science/about/the-fundamentals-of-open-access-and-open-research)
*   Published: 01 July 2025

Digital inequality and neighborhood divides in online social networks in Chicago
================================================================================

*   [Karl Vachuska](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#auth-Karl-Vachuska-Aff1)[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#Aff1)

[_Scientific Reports_](https://www.nature.com/srep)**volume 15**, Article number:21116 (2025) [Cite this article](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#citeas)

*   171 Accesses

*   [Metrics details](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y/metrics)

Abstract
--------

The use of social media has increasingly become a central part of how individuals access and disseminate information. Because the United States is heavily stratified by place, which social media can transcend, some have suggested that social media may alleviate spatial inequalities. The present study builds on this notion by exploring the relationship between Twitter networks and neighborhood and racial inequality in access to information in the city of Chicago. Combining public records with Twitter data, I constructed a geocoded network of 86,336 Chicago Twitter users. My findings indicate that users in Black and Hispanic neighborhoods tend to be less connected to the network, both in terms of out-degree and in-degree, suggesting a potential divide in terms of information access and dissemination. I further identify two mechanisms that likely facilitate the segregation of information between different racial groups: racial homophily in Twitter following and racial selection in message transmission. This study highlights the need to further explore the intersections of race, place, and social media in understanding inequalities in the digital age.

Article Open access 07 December 2023

![Image 5](https://media.springernature.com/w215h120/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44284-024-00032-w/MediaObjects/44284_2024_32_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Explaining changes in US residential segregation through patterns of population change](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44284-024-00032-w?fromPaywallRec=false)

Article 09 February 2024

Introduction
------------

In the realm of sociological inquiry into neighborhoods and urban poverty, few concepts have wielded as profound an impact as that of isolation. Wilson[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR1 "Wilson, W. J. The Truly Disadvantaged: the Inner City, the Underclass, and Public Policy (University of Chicago Press, 1987).") proposes that isolation particularly impacts impoverished non-White urban communities. This estrangement constricts the flow of resources, opportunities, and social connections, thereby hindering upward mobility and perpetuating an unyielding cycle of disadvantage.

However, has the digital age changed things? Tech leaders have repeatedly described the internet as a great equalizer, implying that the near-ubiquity of internet usage has caused the geographical fabric of our world to matter much less than it did in the past. Indeed, evidence suggests that the Internet creates new communities and connects people who would have never crossed paths in the real world[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR2 "Wilson, S. M. & Peterson, L. C. The anthropology of online communities. Annu. Rev. Anthropol. 15, 449–467 (2002).").

Despite the promise of the internet to bridge gaps in network inequalities, however, many recent studies have disagreed with this notion. Scholars have instead found that the structure of online social networks often replicates and reflects the structure of the physical world[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR3 "Tóth, G. et al. Inequality is rising where social network segregation interacts with urban topology. Nat. Commun. 12, 1–9 (2021)."). In particular, research has found that online social networks tend to be highly segregated along racial lines[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR4 "Wimmer, A. & Lewis, K. Beyond and below racial homophily: ERG models of a friendship network documented on Facebook. Am. J. Sociol. 116, 583–642 (2010)."),[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR5 "Hofstra, B., Corten, R., Van Tubergen, F. & Ellison, N. B. Sources of segregation in social networks: a novel approach using Facebook. Am. Sociol. Rev. 82, 625–656 (2017)."). However, little research has explored these questions broadly. Scholarship has especially neglected the neighborhood axes of social network segregation and the actual spread of information. In addition, some evidence surprisingly suggests that non-White users are disproportionally integrated and well-connected on online social media[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR6 "Lewis, K. et al. Tastes, ties, and time: a new social network dataset using Facebook.com. Soc. Netw. 30, 330–342 (2008).").

A social network perspective is useful for understanding the social attributes of neighborhoods and how neighborhood inequality stratifies urban America[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR7 "Sampson, R. J., Morenoff, J. D. & Gannon-Rowley, T. Assessing neighborhood effects: social processes and new directions in research. Annu. Rev. Sociol. 28, 443–478 (2002)."). Social capital is often seen as a key factor in understanding neighborhood disadvantage because it is heavily determined by place of residence and subsequently plays a central role in shaping the resources and opportunities available to residents of a given place. Residents of neighborhoods with low levels of social capital are likely to have less trust in one another and may be less likely to work together to address community problems or advocate for community change[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR8 "Sampson, R. J., Raudenbush, S. W. & Earls, F. Neighborhoods and violent crime: a multilevel study of collective efficacy. Science 277, 918–924 (1997)."). This can make it more difficult for residents to access resources and opportunities, often resulting in a sense of hopelessness and disengagement[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR1 "Wilson, W. J. The Truly Disadvantaged: the Inner City, the Underclass, and Public Policy (University of Chicago Press, 1987).").

Social connections can also be useful for understanding neighborhood social norms and the effects of collective socialization. Isolated sets of social connections may enable social norms that can adversely affect one’s behaviors and life chances[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR9 "Case, A. & Katz, L. F. The company you keep: the effects of family and neighborhood on disadvantaged youths  (1991)."). The impacts of collective socialization may be dependent on thresholds of isolation[10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR10 "Galster, G. An economic efficiency analysis of deconcentrating poverty populations. J. Hous. Econ. 11, 303–329 (2002)."). In contrast, strong social connections within neighborhoods are important for maintaining social cohesion[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR8 "Sampson, R. J., Raudenbush, S. W. & Earls, F. Neighborhoods and violent crime: a multilevel study of collective efficacy. Science 277, 918–924 (1997)."),[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR11 "Sampson, R. J. & Groves, W. B. Community structure and crime: testing social-disorganization theory. Am. J. Sociol. 94, 774–802 (1989)."). Social cohesion can result in greater social control, which can help improve outcomes for neighborhoods and residents[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR8 "Sampson, R. J., Raudenbush, S. W. & Earls, F. Neighborhoods and violent crime: a multilevel study of collective efficacy. Science 277, 918–924 (1997).").

Recent research has further considered everyday mobility patterns in understanding neighborhood effects[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR12 "Levy, B. L., Phillips, N. E. & Sampson, R. J. Triple disadvantage: neighborhood networks of everyday urban mobility and violence in US cities. Am. Sociol. Rev. 85, 925–956 (2020)."),[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR13 "Levy, B. L., Vachuska, K., Subramanian, S. V. & Sampson, R. J. Neighborhood socioeconomic inequality based on everyday mobility predicts COVID-19 infection in San Francisco, Seattle, and Wisconsin. Sci. Adv. 8, eabl3825 (2022)."). Interactions between neighborhoods can provide a more granular method for measuring neighborhood disadvantage, which, in turn, can better predict neighborhood outcomes. The theoretical underpinnings behind these findings rest on the importance of who is interacting with whom in shaping neighborhood processes[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR12 "Levy, B. L., Phillips, N. E. & Sampson, R. J. Triple disadvantage: neighborhood networks of everyday urban mobility and violence in US cities. Am. Sociol. Rev. 85, 925–956 (2020)."). Subsequently, a growing body of research has highlighted the importance of relations between neighborhoods in the study of various neighborhood outcomes, including homicide, police violence, COVID-19 incidences, and adverse birth outcomes[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR12 "Levy, B. L., Phillips, N. E. & Sampson, R. J. Triple disadvantage: neighborhood networks of everyday urban mobility and violence in US cities. Am. Sociol. Rev. 85, 925–956 (2020)."),[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR13 "Levy, B. L., Vachuska, K., Subramanian, S. V. & Sampson, R. J. Neighborhood socioeconomic inequality based on everyday mobility predicts COVID-19 infection in San Francisco, Seattle, and Wisconsin. Sci. Adv. 8, eabl3825 (2022)."),[14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR14 "Vachuska, K. & Brian, L. Neighborhood disadvantage based on everyday mobility predicts intrauterine growth restriction for black and hispanic births in California. In PAA 2022 Annual Meeting  (2022)."),[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR15 "Vachuska, K. & Brian, L. Neighborhood Racial and Economic Composition Predicts Risk of Police Killing. In PAA 2022 Annual Meeting (2022).").

In terms of broader processes of social stratification, neighborhoods, and social networks have been shown to be highly relevant to intergenerational mobility. Chetty and colleagues[16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR16 "Chetty, R. et al. Social capital I: measurement and associations with economic mobility. Nature 608, 108–121 (2022).") have shown that the degree of economic diversity in social connections is an extremely strong predictor of upward intergenerational mobility. Notably, other measures of social capital, such as network cohesiveness or civic engagement, are relatively weak predictors of upward intergenerational mobility. Chetty et al.[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR17 "Chetty, R. et al. Social capital II: determinants of economic connectedness. Nature 608, 122–134 (2022).") theorizes that social connections between individuals of low and high socioeconomic status are key to upward intergenerational mobility, as these ties “can be valuable for transferring information, shaping aspirations and providing mentorship or job referrals.”

Ultimately, social networks can significantly impact the resources and opportunities available to people living in a given neighborhood. Social networks can influence people’s access to information, which can impact various processes related to social stratification. A body of sociology literature has specifically studied the implications of racial inequality in social networks through the application of job searches. Pedulla and Pager[18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR18 "Pedulla, D. S. & Devah, P. Race and networks in the job search process. Am. Sociol. Rev. 84, 983–1012 (2019).") found that racial inequality in social networks operates over multiple layers. For example, while Black men utilize their social networks at similar rates to White men, they get far worse returns. Pedulla and Pager decompose this inequality into differences in the performance of social networks resulting from disparate network placement and network mobilization. Not only do jobs found through networks tend to be better positions, but there is also a wage premium for finding a job through networks versus a formal search process[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR19 "McDonald, S. Network effects across the earnings distribution: payoffs to visible and invisible job finding assistance. Soc. Sci. Res. 49, 299–313 (2015).").

Although past research has investigated neighborhood networks, much of it has conceptualized simple ego networks (networks surrounding single individuals) and not examined neighborhood networks as broader systems of social ties over multiple degrees of connection. A large body of network research suggests that multiple degrees of connection are key for the greatest information flow and for understanding tie formation and network development[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR20 "Burt, R. S. Structural holes and good ideas. Am. J. Sociol. 110, 349–399 (2004)."),[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR21 "Faraj, S. & Steven, L. J. Network exchange patterns in online communities. Org. Sci. 22, 1464–1480 (2011)."). As such, understanding the broader structure of social ties between different socioeconomic and racial groups is crucial to understanding information flow and how social relations are structured.

Ultimately, understanding inequalities in social networks is key to understanding broader racial inequality because social networks play a crucial role in shaping the resources and opportunities available to individuals. Inequalities in online social networks can arise from several separate, but interrelated forms of inequality. Access inequality in actual internet access can shape who even has access to online social networks, and has been previously documented to have a racial and socioeconomic component to it[22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR22 "Rodriguez-Elliott, S. & Karl, V. Measuring the digital divide: a neighborhood-level analysis of racial inequality in internet speed during the COVID-19 pandemic. Societies 13, 92 (2023)."). Even given equal access, skill inequality in terms of digital literacy may also exacerbate inequalities in who actually joins online social networks[23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR23 "Hsieh, Y. P. Online social networking skills: the social affordances approach to digital inequality. First Monday 17, 145 (2012)."). Across users on a social network platform, participation inequality may generate more systemic inequalities in the nature and type of content on the platform[24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR24 "Schradie, J. The trend of class, race, and ethnicity in social media inequality: who still cannot afford to blog? Inf. Commun. Soc. 15, 555–571 (2012)."). Finally, visibility inequality in terms of who is seen and followed, including differences in the number and quality of social connections as well as differential access to important actors or organizations, may generate inequalities in how information is shared on the platform[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR25 "Zhu, L. & Kristina, L. Attention inequality in social media. Preprint arXiv :160107200 (2016)."). While this particular study is unable to explicitly decompose the contribution of each of these forms of inequality, by descriptively documenting overall inequality and segregation in online social networks, I aim to demonstrate the cumulative effect of these inequalities.

In this paper, I investigate four questions related to neighborhoods and networks. Access inequality and skill inequality would suggest that marginalized racial groups are less likely to be on social media to begin with. Research, however, generally suggests that the racial demographics of Twitter roughly approximate the racial demographics of the United States as a whole[26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR26 "Pew Research Center. Sizing up Twitter users. Pew Research Center: Internet & Technology  (2019). 
                  https://www.pewresearch.org/internet/2019/04/24/sizing-up-twitter-users/
                  
                "). Regardless, very little research has approached this question from a spatial perspective. Are individuals in Black and Hispanic neighborhoods more or less likely to be on Twitter? Visibility inequality suggests that even among those on Twitter, users from marginalized groups may be on the margins of the platform. Are users from poorer and more non-White neighborhoods less well-connected? While past research has documented racial segregation on social media[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR6 "Lewis, K. et al. Tastes, ties, and time: a new social network dataset using Facebook.com. Soc. Netw. 30, 330–342 (2008)."),[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR27 "Cesare, N., Hedwig, L., Tyler, M. & Emma, S. S. Redrawing the ' Color Line’: examining racial segregation in associative networks on Twitter. Preprint at arXiv:1705.04401 (2017)."), little research has explored online segregation from a neighborhood perspective. Importantly, analyzing segregation from a neighborhood perspective can disentangle segregation attributable to spatial proximity from broader racial assortativity.

Both offline factors, and algorithms specific to the Twitter platform may play a role in exacerbating racial segregation on the platform. Offline social networks tend to be segregated spatially[28](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR28 "Hipp, J. R. & Andrew, J. P. The simultaneous effect of social distance and physical distance on the formation of neighborhood ties. City Commun. 8, 5–25 (2009)."), so to the extent that online social networks resemble offline social networks, this factor alone could drive social media segregation. Research has indicated that only about 15% of Twitter users followers are offline connections however[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR27 "Cesare, N., Hedwig, L., Tyler, M. & Emma, S. S. Redrawing the ' Color Line’: examining racial segregation in associative networks on Twitter. Preprint at arXiv:1705.04401 (2017)."), suggesting if segregation exists on Twitter, the source of it must be secondary to offline connections. Other factors, such as educational inequality, occupational inequality, and digital literacy inequality may reinforce the isolation of certain groups of users on Twitter[29](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR29 "Billon, M., Jorge, C. & Fernando, L. Do educational inequalities affect internet use? An analysis for developed and developing countries. Telemat Inf. 58, 101521 (2021)."). Additionally, algorithmic bias or platform-specific usage habits may also reinforce segregation on social media[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR27 "Cesare, N., Hedwig, L., Tyler, M. & Emma, S. S. Redrawing the ' Color Line’: examining racial segregation in associative networks on Twitter. Preprint at arXiv:1705.04401 (2017)."),[30](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR30 "Bartley, N., Andres, A., Emilio, F. & Kristina, L. Auditing algorithmic bias on twitter. In Proceedings of the 13th ACM Web Science Conference (2021)."). This past literature on racial segregation on Twitter motivates research on how Twitter segregation maps onto spatial segregation: Are Twitter ties less likely to form between individuals in racially or socioeconomically dissimilar neighborhoods? While much research suggests that this is the case, research is mixed in attributing racial neighborhood divisions to socioeconomic divisions, and other research has simply attributed divisions between neighborhoods to the sheer distance between them[28](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR28 "Hipp, J. R. & Andrew, J. P. The simultaneous effect of social distance and physical distance on the formation of neighborhood ties. City Commun. 8, 5–25 (2009).").

The Twitter algorithm has been documented as skewing the popularity and novelty of the content users see, as well as increasing inequality in exposure to different content[30](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR30 "Bartley, N., Andres, A., Emilio, F. & Kristina, L. Auditing algorithmic bias on twitter. In Proceedings of the 13th ACM Web Science Conference (2021)."). Since the Twitter algorithm functions by exposing users to accounts and content that they predict the user will engage with, the platform may inadvertently recreate segregation[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR27 "Cesare, N., Hedwig, L., Tyler, M. & Emma, S. S. Redrawing the ' Color Line’: examining racial segregation in associative networks on Twitter. Preprint at arXiv:1705.04401 (2017)."). Some past research suggests that racial segregation is very high on Twitter, despite most Twitter users not having offline relationships with users they follow, indicating a combination of user choice and algorithmic bias recreates segregation on the site[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR27 "Cesare, N., Hedwig, L., Tyler, M. & Emma, S. S. Redrawing the ' Color Line’: examining racial segregation in associative networks on Twitter. Preprint at arXiv:1705.04401 (2017)."). While past research suggests that White Twitter users have higher follower counts[31](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR31 "Messias, J., Pantelis, V. & Fabrício, B. White, man, and highly followed: gender and race inequalities in Twitter. In Proceedings of the International Conference on Web Intelligence (2017)."), other research suggests no algorithmic bias against Black users[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR32 "Belli, L. et al. County-level algorithmic audit of racial bias in twitter’s home timeline. Preprint arXiv :221108667 (2022)."). Feedback loops between following patterns and content visibility may amplify the segregation of users on the platform. This past research motivates my third question: is information less likely to flow between individuals in racially dissimilar neighborhoods? Little research has investigated this question, but it is of central importance to understanding neighborhood and racial inequalities in information flow and access.

I conclude by combining aspects of the prior questions into a single case study: Does racial inequality exist in how well individuals are connected to important public institutions? Past research has revealed substantial inequalities in access to public health information[33](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR33 "Taylor-Clark, K. A., Kasisomayajula, V. & Robert, J. B. Communication inequalities during public health disasters: Katrina’s wake. Health Commun. 25, 221–229 (2010)."), as well as also suggested that Black and Hispanic Americans trust health information from the internet about as much as non-Hispanic White Americans[34](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR34 "Viswanath, K. & Leland, K. A. Race, ethnicity, language, social class, and health communication inequalities: a nationally-representative cross-sectional study. PloS One 6, e14550 (2011)."). This is potentially problematic in that social media, and particularly Twitter, have been documented as having high rates of health misinformation[35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR35 "Broniatowski, D. A. et al. Weaponized health communication: Twitter bots and Russian Trolls amplify the vaccine debate. Am. J. Public. Health 108, 1378–1384 (2018)."). Health organization bodies may be a critical source of credible health information on social media[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-02261-y#ref-CR36 "Park, H., Bryan, H. R. & Myoung-Gi, C. Tweeting as health communication: health organizations’ use of Twitter for health promotion and public engagement. J. Health Commun. 21, 188–198 (2016)."), so the extent to which different racial groups are exposed to information from public health bodies on Twitter is greatly important for determining equitable information

# Inequality and cooperation in social networks

[*Scientific Reports*](/srep)
**volume 12**, Article number: 6789 (2022)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

6315 Accesses

18 Citations

2 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

Social networks are fundamental to the broad scale cooperation observed in human populations. But by structuring the flow of benefits from cooperation, networks also create and sustain macro-level inequalities. Here we ask how two aspects of inequality shape the evolution of cooperation in dynamic social networks. Results from a crowdsourced experiment (N = 1080) show that inequality alters the distribution of cooperation within networks such that participants engage in more costly cooperation with their wealthier partners in order to maintain more valuable connections to them. Inequality also influences network dynamics, increasing the tendency for participants to seek wealthier partners, resulting in structural network change. These processes aggregate to alter network structures and produce greater system-level inequality. The findings thus shed critical light on how networks serve as both boon and barrier to macro-level human flourishing.

## Introduction

Both cooperation[1](#ref-CR1 "Ohtsuki, H., Hauert, C., Lieberman, E. & Nowak, M. A. A simple rule for the evolution of cooperation on graphs and social networks. Nature 441, 502–505 (2006)."),[2](#ref-CR2 "DiMaggio, P. & Garip, F. Network effects and social inequality. Ann. Rev. Sociol. 38, 93–118 (2012)."),[3](#ref-CR3 "Apicella, C. L., Marlowe, F. W., Fowler, J. H. & Christakis, N. A. Social networks and cooperation in hunter-gatherers. Nature 481, 497–501 (2012)."),[4](#ref-CR4 "Balliet, D. & Van Lange, P. A. Trust, punishment, and cooperation across 18 societies: A meta-analysis. Perspect. Psychol. Sci. 8, 363–379 (2013)."),[5](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR5 "Rand, D. G. & Nowak, M. A. Human cooperation. Trends Cogn. Sci. 17, 413–425 (2013).") and resource inequality[6](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR6 "Piketty, T. & Saez, E. Inequality in the long run. Science 344, 838–843 (2014)."),[7](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR7 "Mulder, M. B. et al. Intergenerational wealth transmission and the dynamics of inequality in small-scale societies. Science 326, 682–688 (2009).") are universal but variable across human societies. Although several studies have suggested that variation in material wealth inequality and cooperation may be linked[2](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR2 "DiMaggio, P. & Garip, F. Network effects and social inequality. Ann. Rev. Sociol. 38, 93–118 (2012)."),[8](#ref-CR8 "Nishi, A., Shirado, H., Rand, D. G. & Christakis, N. A. Inequality and visibility of wealth in experimental social networks. Nature 526, 426–429 (2015)."),[9](#ref-CR9 "Heap, S. P., Hargreaves, A. R. & Stoddard, B. V. Endowment inequality in public goods games: A re-examination. Econ. Lett. 146, 4–7 (2016)."),[10](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR10 "Hauser, O. P., Hilbe, C., Chatterjee, K. & Nowak, M. A. Social dilemmas among unequals. Nature 572, 524–527 (2019)."), we currently know little about how inequality in material wealth or endowments impacts cooperation[8](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR8 "Nishi, A., Shirado, H., Rand, D. G. & Christakis, N. A. Inequality and visibility of wealth in experimental social networks. Nature 526, 426–429 (2015).") or how cooperation, in turn, influences inequalities[11](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR11 "Tsvetkova, M. The effects of reputation on inequality in network cooperation games. Philos. Trans. R. Soc. B 376, 20200299 (2021)."). We study the bidirectional effects of cooperation and inequality in dynamic networks, where ties between alters represent opportunities to cooperate[8](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR8 "Nishi, A., Shirado, H., Rand, D. G. & Christakis, N. A. Inequality and visibility of wealth in experimental social networks. Nature 526, 426–429 (2015)."),[12](#ref-CR12 "Rand, D. G., Arbesman, S. & Christakis, N. A. Dynamic social networks promote cooperation in experiments with humans. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 108, 19193–19198 (2011)."),[13](#ref-CR13 "Wang, J., Suri, S. & Watts, D. J. Cooperation and assortativity with dynamic partner updating. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 109, 14363–14368 (2012)."),[14](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR14 "Melamed, D., Harrell, A. & Simpson, B. Cooperation, clustering, and assortative mixing in dynamic networks. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 115, 951–956 (2018).").

Two interrelated bases of inequality are likely fundamental for cooperation in social networks. First, inequality in *wealth* may lead to differences in self-reliance, which decreases the tendency for the wealthy to cooperate or form ties to new partners, especially with the poor. Some evidence supports the contention that visible inequality results in the wealthy being less cooperative[8](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR8 "Nishi, A., Shirado, H., Rand, D. G. & Christakis, N. A. Inequality and visibility of wealth in experimental social networks. Nature 526, 426–429 (2015)."),[9](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR9 "Heap, S. P., Hargreaves, A. R. & Stoddard, B. V. Endowment inequality in public goods games: A re-examination. Econ. Lett. 146, 4–7 (2016)."). Alternatively, wealth may lead to noblesse oblige, leading the rich to be more generous in their interactions with their less fortunate network partners. There is theory and evidence supporting this view as well[10](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR10 "Hauser, O. P., Hilbe, C., Chatterjee, K. & Nowak, M. A. Social dilemmas among unequals. Nature 572, 524–527 (2019)."). We refer to any tendency for greater wealth to affect one’s behavior as *baseline wealth effects*.

We argue that wealth will affect both cooperation and network dynamics beyond its effects on the holder of wealth. Here we test the argument that the tendency for people to derive greater benefits from interactions with wealthy partners[10](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR10 "Hauser, O. P., Hilbe, C., Chatterjee, K. & Nowak, M. A. Social dilemmas among unequals. Nature 572, 524–527 (2019)."),[15](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR15 "Lin, N. Social networks and status attainment. Ann. Rev. Sociol. 25, 467–487 (1999).") can generate differences in cooperation, altering network dynamics and exacerbating existing inequalities in networks. That is, a key starting point for our investigation is that cooperators who possess more material wealth (or other valuable resources like technology or knowledge) can generate larger material benefits for their network partners than cooperators with access to less material wealth. Thus, collaborations with individuals with more wealth or other valuable resources (e.g., greater human capital) generally result in higher overall outcomes. For example, one will likely benefit more from investing in a business venture with a wealthy partner than a poor partner, or by working on a project with an experienced vs. inexperienced collaborator. Following related work, we refer to this second aspect of inequality as *wealth productivity effects*[10](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR10 "Hauser, O. P., Hilbe, C., Chatterjee, K. & Nowak, M. A. Social dilemmas among unequals. Nature 572, 524–527 (2019).").

Critically, however, interactions with the wealthy are only more productive if the wealthy are cooperative. One does not benefit from collaborating with a wealthy or experienced partner who freerides on one’s efforts while contributing nothing of their own. We expect that wealth productivity effects will lead to more cooperation with the wealthy, both to maintain ties to them and to bring about higher levels of cooperation from them. Thus, when we account for wealth productivity effects, we expect that these higher levels of preferential attachment to the rich and cooperation with them will lead to “rich-get-richer effects.” This, in turn, will result in increased network-level inequality. This is consistent with recent work[16](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR16 "Hackel, L. M. & Zaki, J. Propagation of economic inequality through reciprocity and reputation. Psychol. Sci. 29, 604–613 (2018).") showing that the greater resources of the wealthy allow them to produce larger benefits for interaction partners for any given level of cooperation. Those interaction partners, in turn, attribute higher levels of cooperativeness to the wealthy than their (equally cooperative) poorer counterparts, leading the wealthy to gain more reputational benefits, which can then lead to increased monetary rewards in downstream interactions.

Summing up, we study not only the effects of wealth inequality and productivity on cooperation in dynamic networks, but also how those factors lead to concentration of both material wealth and social wealth (i.e., social ties). More specifically, we conducted a large-scale behavioral experiment using human subjects to answer several interrelated questions about how inequality shapes cooperation, network dynamics, and macro-level inequalities: (1) How do baseline wealth and wealth productivity affect cooperation in networks? (2) Does wealth productivity increase cooperation because participants give more to wealthy partners? (3) Does wealth productivity lead to preferential attachment to the rich? If so, does inequality in network degree increase? And, (4) Do the effects of inequality on cooperation and network dynamics combine to increase network-level inequality? That is, does wealth productivity lead to “rich get richer” effects by shaping who cooperates with whom?

A total of 1080 participants were embedded in 40 dynamic networks (average initial network size = 27; “[Methods](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Sec7)”). Initial networks were random (Erdös-Rényi) graphs, with a density of 0.167 or about 4 ties each. Each network tie represented an opportunity to interact in an iterated prisoner’s dilemma (PD). In each round, each participant made a single decision to give 0 to 50 monetary units (MUs; in ten-unit increments) to all of their alters[8](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR8 "Nishi, A., Shirado, H., Rand, D. G. & Christakis, N. A. Inequality and visibility of wealth in experimental social networks. Nature 526, 426–429 (2015)."),[12](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR12 "Rand, D. G., Arbesman, S. & Christakis, N. A. Dynamic social networks promote cooperation in experiments with humans. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 108, 19193–19198 (2011)."),[13](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR13 "Wang, J., Suri, S. & Watts, D. J. Cooperation and assortativity with dynamic partner updating. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 109, 14363–14368 (2012)."), where 0 represented full defection and 50 represented maximal cooperation. Specifically, consistent with a PD incentive structure, any given person in an interaction benefited most when they gave nothing and the other gave maximally (the "T" payoff); joint benefit was highest when both gave maximally (the "R" payoff); joint benefit was lowest when both gave nothing (the "P" payoff); and the worst individual outcome alter gave nothing and ego gave maximally and (the "S" payoff). As a PD, T > R > P > S. After each round, participants were told how much each of their partners had cooperated and how much they benefited from each partner’s cooperation.

Participants could sever one tie and propose a new one every three rounds. Ties could be severed unilaterally, but proposed new ties required approval by the selected other. Participants were not informed that tie updates occurred regularly. When given the opportunity to add ties, participants saw alters’ participant ID, their endowment, and how much that alters’ partners had received from them in the previous round ([Supplementary Materials](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#MOESM1)). The study lasted 19 rounds. But to avoid end game effects, participants were not told this.

Our experiment was a 2 × 2 design. We manipulated whether there was baseline wealth inequality in the initial endowment distribution and whether network relations were characterized by wealth productivity. In the baseline wealth equality condition, all participants received 1000 MUs at the beginning of the study[12](#ref-CR12 "Rand, D. G., Arbesman, S. & Christakis, N. A. Dynamic social networks promote cooperation in experiments with humans. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 108, 19193–19198 (2011)."),[13](#ref-CR13 "Wang, J., Suri, S. & Watts, D. J. Cooperation and assortativity with dynamic partner updating. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 109, 14363–14368 (2012)."),[14](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR14 "Melamed, D., Harrell, A. & Simpson, B. Cooperation, clustering, and assortative mixing in dynamic networks. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 115, 951–956 (2018)."). In the baseline wealth inequality condition, the endowment distribution had an average of 1000 MUs, but a Gini coefficient of 0.3, comparable to Austria, Poland and Hungary[17](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR17 "World Bank, World Development Indicators. 
                  https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/SI.POV.GINI
                  
                .") and substantially less than the U.S. (Gini = 0.41). Endowments were randomly assigned to participants. Participants could see the endowments of those to whom they were connected. They could also see the endowments of non-partners in the tie selection phase. Thus, they had a rough sense of the distribution of endowments.

Our key manipulation, wealth productivity, was whether the effect of alter’s cooperation on ego’s outcome was a function of alters’ endowment. As is standard in the literature[12](#ref-CR12 "Rand, D. G., Arbesman, S. & Christakis, N. A. Dynamic social networks promote cooperation in experiments with humans. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 108, 19193–19198 (2011)."),[13](#ref-CR13 "Wang, J., Suri, S. & Watts, D. J. Cooperation and assortativity with dynamic partner updating. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 109, 14363–14368 (2012)."),[14](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR14 "Melamed, D., Harrell, A. & Simpson, B. Cooperation, clustering, and assortative mixing in dynamic networks. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 115, 951–956 (2018)."), in the control condition, each MU given to alter was doubled. In the wealth productivity treatment condition, however, the amount each alter received from ego’s cooperation was a function of ego’s endowment: the larger ego’s endowment, the more the alter benefited from ego’s cooperation. Figure [1](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Fig1) illustrates how ego’s endowment and cooperation level shape partner outcomes. Conceptually the wealth productivity condition captures a basic feature of much of social life: interacting with wealthier partners (or partners who are higher in human capital) can bring larger benefits than interacting with less wealthy partners, but only if those wealthy partners are cooperative. As in real world interactions, the wealth productivity manipulation infuses value (endowments) into the social system when wealthy actors are cooperative. But, as a conservative test of our arguments, we adjusted the endowments to remove these effects when investigating network-level inequality ([Supplementary Materials](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#MOESM1)).

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-022-10733-8/MediaObjects/41598_2022_10733_Fig1_HTML.png)

Illustration of our wealth productivity manipulation. As alters endowment increases, so does the amount ego receives from varying levels of alter’s cooperation (10, 30, and 50).

## Results

### Cooperation

Figure [2](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Fig2)A shows average rates of cooperation through time by experimental condition. Consistent with past work on dynamic networks, we find that cooperation increases quickly[12](#ref-CR12 "Rand, D. G., Arbesman, S. & Christakis, N. A. Dynamic social networks promote cooperation in experiments with humans. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 108, 19193–19198 (2011)."),[13](#ref-CR13 "Wang, J., Suri, S. & Watts, D. J. Cooperation and assortativity with dynamic partner updating. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 109, 14363–14368 (2012)."),[14](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR14 "Melamed, D., Harrell, A. & Simpson, B. Cooperation, clustering, and assortative mixing in dynamic networks. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 115, 951–956 (2018).") and that initial endowment inequality induces additional cooperation[8](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR8 "Nishi, A., Shirado, H., Rand, D. G. & Christakis, N. A. Inequality and visibility of wealth in experimental social networks. Nature 526, 426–429 (2015)."),[9](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR9 "Heap, S. P., Hargreaves, A. R. & Stoddard, B. V. Endowment inequality in public goods games: A re-examination. Econ. Lett. 146, 4–7 (2016)."). Wealth productivity enhances cooperation early on when there is endowment inequality, and increases cooperation in later rounds when there is initial endowment equality, a pattern we describe in more detail below.

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-022-10733-8/MediaObjects/41598_2022_10733_Fig2_HTML.png)

Average (**A**) cooperation and (**B**) inequality (Gini coefficient) by experimental condition through time. *No Productivity, Equality* indicates no wealth productivity and baseline endowment equality. *Productivity, Inequality* indicates wealth productivity and baseline endowment inequality.

Table [1](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Tab1) presents the results from four models of cooperation at three different levels of analysis. In Model 1, we examine how our experimental manipulations are related to average rates of cooperation over the last four rounds, i.e., after cooperation rates have stabilized. Consistent with some past work[8](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR8 "Nishi, A., Shirado, H., Rand, D. G. & Christakis, N. A. Inequality and visibility of wealth in experimental social networks. Nature 526, 426–429 (2015).") we find that initial baseline inequality promotes cooperation in our social systems. While the wealth productivity manipulation increases cooperation, it is not significant; as we report below, however, this is due to ceiling effects on cooperation specifically in these latter rounds of our study.

Next we look at how cooperation unfolds in networks over time. Here we find that both of our inequality manipulations interact with time to shape cooperation trajectories. Model 2 of Table [1](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Tab1) presents these parameter estimates and Fig. [3](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Fig3) illustrates the implications of the interaction effects. In networks with an initially equal baseline endowment distribution, we observe that wealth productivity has a positive effect only once inequality emerges in later rounds. On the other hand, in networks with an initially unequal baseline endowment distribution we observe that wealth productivity has a positive effect early on that is diminished later in the study. This is likely due to a ceiling effect, given the very high rates of cooperation in later rounds. This diminishment at the end of the study is why we do not observe a main effect of wealth productivity at the network level of analysis (i.e., Model 1).

![figure 3](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-022-10733-8/MediaObjects/41598_2022_10733_Fig3_HTML.png)

Marginal cooperation illustrating the interaction between wealth productivity, baseline inequality and round.

Building on the insights in Fig. [3](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Fig3), we find that network-level inequality, as measured by the Gini coefficient, moderates the interaction between our two experimental manipulations. Further, this interaction is not moderated by round ([Supplementary Materials](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#MOESM1)). Model 3 in Table [1](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Tab1) presents a summary of the parameter estimates and Fig. [4](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Fig4) presents marginal means from the model to illustrate the interaction effect. We find that wealth productivity promotes cooperation after the emergence of inequality in initially equal systems. And in part due to the ceiling effect described above, wealth productivity only has a small positive effect on cooperation in initially unequal systems. That is, our data suggests that the presence of wealth productivity in human social relations promotes cooperation when there is inequality in the system (and when network dynamics have not induced a cooperation ceiling).

![figure 4](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-022-10733-8/MediaObjects/41598_2022_10733_Fig4_HTML.png)

Marginal means from Model 3 of Table [1](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Tab1) illustrating the interaction between wealth productivity, baseline (manipulated) inequality, and observed network-level inequality.

In terms of why cooperation is higher in the wealth productivity condition, we predicted participants would give more to wealthier partners in the presence of wealth productivity effects. This is because investing more in “higher value” relations follows from a greater incentive to maintain ties with those relations that have the potential to benefit them more[18](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR18 "Axelrod, R. & Hamilton, W. D. The evolution of cooperation. Science 211, 1390–1396 (1981)."),[19](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#ref-CR19 "Van Veelen, M., García, J., Rand, D. G. & Nowak, M. A. Direct reciprocity in structured populations. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 109, 9929–9934 (2012)."). As illustrated in Fig. [5](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Fig5), regardless of whether the networks entailed initial baseline endowment inequality, we find that participants in the control condition give less to their wealthier alters, whereas participants in the wealth productivity condition gave more to their wealthy alters. Relative to the control, wealth productivity increases cooperation when alters are relatively rich (Table [1](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Tab1), Model 4, b = 1.11, se = 0.45, *p* = 0.02).

![figure 5](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-022-10733-8/MediaObjects/41598_2022_10733_Fig5_HTML.png)

Marginal means from Model 4 of Table [1](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Tab1) illustrating the interaction between wealth productivity and average alter endowment.

### Network dynamics

Figure [6](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Fig6) shows descriptive statistics for network dynamics. Most network change happened in earlier rounds, as indicated by fewer decisions to drop a partner and select a new one in later rounds (see slope declines in Fig. [6](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Fig6)A). In terms of network exclusion (Fig. [6](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Fig6)B), participants appear to be consistently at risk throughout the study, with heightened risk in the wealth productivity and endowment inequality condition (purple line). Below we report results of statistical models adjusting for participant behaviors on condition-level effects on network dynamics that confirm this.

![figure 6](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-022-10733-8/MediaObjects/41598_2022_10733_Fig6_HTML.png)

Cumulative (**A**) tie updates and (**B**) network isolates by experimental condition through time.

When altering their ties, participants decided whether to drop an alter (Fig. [6](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Fig6)A). If they elected to drop an alter, they then indicated which alter to drop. Participants could then decide which new alter to add. If the proposed tie was confirmed by the alter, a new tie was formed. (That is, severing ties was a unilateral decision, but forming new ties was bilateral). Aside from these individual decisions, this process also resulted in some participants being excluded from the entire network (Fig. [6](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Fig6)B). After adjusting for the effect of time and cooperation ([Supplementary Material](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#MOESM1)), we find that participants in the baseline endowment inequality condition were less likely to drop alters (Table [S1](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#MOESM1), Model 2: b = − 0.35, se = 0.11, *p* = 0.002), but that those in the wealth productivity condition did not differ from the control. Conditional on dropping a partner, net of cooperativeness, participants were more likely to drop wealthier alters. While this effect is diminished in the wealth productivity condition, the decrease is not statistically significant (Table [S2](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#MOESM1), Model 2: b = − 0.069, se = 0.069, *p* = 0.32).

Wealth productivity has a strong effect on new partner selection, however. While participants selected wealthier alters in the control condition (Table [S3](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#MOESM1), Model2: b = 0.288, se = 0.036, *p* < 0.001), the effect of alter wealth was more than twice as strong in the wealth productivity condition (b = 0.295, se = 0.050, *p* < 0.001). Thus, while decisions to sever a tie were primarily driven by alter’s cooperation, the formation of new partners was based on the prospective alter’s cooperativeness and wealth. Most importantly, wealth particularly mattered when it had an impact on ego’s welfare, i.e., in the wealth productivity condition. To illustrate, Fig. [7](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Fig7) shows the marginal change in number of partners as a function of participant wealth and whether they were in the wealth productivity condition. Most change is negative since networks got smaller, on average, over time. We find no difference by condition in the decrease in number of partners for relatively poor participants, but wealthier participants in the wealth productivity condition lose significantly fewer partners on average than wealthier participants in the control condition. This preferential attachment to rich alters resulted in structural change to the networks, with networks in the wealth productivity condition acquiring higher inequality in the degree distribution than those in control networks (Fig. [S7](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#MOESM1)). This means that not only was wealth increasingly concentrated; relationships were increasingly concentrated as well.

![figure 7](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-022-10733-8/MediaObjects/41598_2022_10733_Fig7_HTML.png)

Marginal change in number of partners illustrating the interaction between participant baseline wealth and wealth productivity.

Above we focused on the number of ties among active members of our networks. Another important consequence of these network dynamics is that 15.5% of participants became isolated from their network. These participants were therefore excluded from the analyses above. Here, we focus on the hazard of having no ties, i.e., becoming excluded. We find that despite cooperating at higher rates (Fig. [S9](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#MOESM1)), participants in the combined wealth productivity and baseline inequality condition were significantly less likely to complete all rounds before becoming isolated. Figure [8](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Fig8) presents survival probabilities by experimental condition for each tie update opportunity from a Cox proportional hazards model ([Supplementary Material](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#MOESM1)). As illustrated, after adjusting for the fact that those in the wealth productivity/baseline inequality condition cooperated at higher rates than other isolates, they are still at increased risk of network exclusion.

![figure 8](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-022-10733-8/MediaObjects/41598_2022_10733_Fig8_HTML.png)

Cox Proportional Hazard model survival probabilities at each network update by experimental condition. Participant endowments and cooperation (time-varying covariates) are set to their means.

### Inequality

Figure [2](/articles/s41598-022-10733-8#Fig2)B shows average inequality in endowments, as measured by the Gini coefficient, through time by experimental condition. Consi

enriched_points.point_title: Prevalence of online and offline networks
enriched_points.expanded_content: Social networks exist both online and offline, with individuals increasingly relying on digital platforms for communication, yet offline connections remain crucial for community engagement and support.
Network composition varies widely, reflecting socioeconomic, racial, and geographic characteristics, which can lead to segregation and unequal access to information and resources within communities.

Social networks serve as conduits for accessing resources, opportunities, and information, with disparities in network connectivity often reflecting broader social inequalities and affecting individuals' life chances.
